chinese_text: 古之善為士者，微妙玄通，深不可識。
translation_text: The skilful masters (of the Dao) in old times, with a subtle and exquisite penetration, comprehended its mysteries, and were deep (also) so as to elude men's knowledge.

chinese_text: 夫唯不可識，故強為之容。
translation_text: As they were thus beyond men's knowledge, I will make an effort to describe of what sort they appeared to be.
Shrinking looked they like those who wade through a stream in winter;
irresolute like those who are afraid of all around them;
grave like a guest (in awe of his host);
evanescent like ice that is melting away;
unpretentious like wood that has not been fashioned into anything;
vacant like a valley, and dull like muddy water.

chinese_text: 混兮其若濁。孰能濁以靜之徐清？
translation_text: Who can (make) the muddy water (clear)? Let it be still, and it will gradually become clear. Who can secure the condition of rest?

chinese_text: 孰能安以久動之徐生？保此道者不欲盈，
translation_text: Let movement go on, and the condition of rest will gradually arise. They who preserve this method of the Dao do not wish to be full (of themselves).

chinese_text: 夫唯不盈，故能蔽不新成。
translation_text: It is through their not being full of themselves that they can afford to seem worn and not appear new and complete.
